title: Strong light-matter interactions in hybrid polaritonic systems
authors: Ben Johns, Andrea Schirato, Federico Toffoletti, Tommaso Giovannini, Mirko Vanzan, Margherita Marsili, Giovanni Parolin, Giulia Dall'Osto, Ajay Kumar Poonia, Chiara Cappelli, Francesca Baletto, Stefano Corni, Elisabetta Collini, Margherita Maiuri, Nicolò Maccaferri
abstract: Strong light-matter coupling gives rise to polaritons - hybrid excitations whose mixed photonic and matter character enables control over optical, electronic and chemical properties. This Feature Article surveys the main architectures supporting polariton formation, including photonic microcavities, plasmonic nanostructures, open cavities and metasurfaces, and outlines how inorganic semiconductors, organic aggregates and hybrid systems access strong and ultrastrong coupling. Key phenomena such as coherent dynamics, vibronic interactions, dark-state reservoirs and polariton-mediated energy and electron transport are discussed, together with the experimental and theoretical tools used to study them. We highlight examples where strong coupling modifies charge transport, energy flow and chemical reactivity, and we summarize emerging regimes, including intermediate and dark-strong coupling, that broaden the landscape of hybrid light-matter physics.
summary: This feature article surveys the landscape of hybrid light-matter systems, focusing on how engineering electromagnetic environments via strong coupling can control material properties. It covers a wide range of platforms from microcavities to plasmonic nanostructures and discusses the resulting impacts on energy and charge transport.
main_problem: Understanding and controlling the optical, electronic, and chemical properties of matter by engineering the electromagnetic environment through strong light-matter interactions in hybrid polaritonic systems.
main_result: The article surveys how various architectures (microcavities, plasmonic nanostructures, metasurals) and materials (organic, inorganic, 2D) enable access to strong and ultrastrong coupling regimes, highlighting how these interactions modify charge transport, energy flow, and chemical reactivity.
method: The paper reviews experimental techniques like Fourier microscopy and 2D electronic spectroscopy alongside theoretical/computational approaches including multiscale quantum/classical models, DFT, and PCM-NP.
model_or_system: Hybrid polaritonic and plexcitonic systems consisting of excitons (Frenkel or Wannier-Mott) coupled to optical modes in photonic microcavities, plasmonic nanostructures, or metasurfaces.
key_observables: Rabi splitting, Rabi oscillations, polariton dispersion, Hopfield coefficients, and photoluminescence intensity.
important_parameters: Q-factor, mode volume, exciton-cavity detuning, coupling regimes (strong, ultrastrong, dark-strong), and dissipation rates.
main_assumptions: The review notes limitations such as the difficulty in accurately determining Rabi splitting when comparable to linewidths and the challenges of modeling complex, large-scale hybrid environments.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a conceptual overview of building blocks, mechanisms, tools, and applications; Figure 2 shows schematic representations of specific plexcitonic colloidal nanohybrids; Figure 4 demonstrates Fourier microscopy on a WS2/TDBC hybrid.
paper_structure: The article progresses from fundamental building blocks (cavities/nanostructures) to physical mechanisms (coherent dynamics, vibronic interactions), then to experimental/theoretical tools, and finally to emerging applications and challenges.
why_interesting: It provides a comprehensive overview of cavity QED-like phenomena (strong/ultrastrong coupling) applied to condensed matter, specifically focusing on non-equilibrium dynamics, vibronic-polariton states, and the interplay between quantum and classical descriptions in nanoplasmonics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Conceptual overview of hybrid polaritonic systems and their key ingredients. The figure summarises representative photonic and plasmonic building blocks enabling strong light-matter coupling (top left), key physical mechanisms emerging from polariton formation (top right), experimental and theoretical approaches employed to investigate polaritons (bottom left), and selected application areas enabled by polaritonic effects (bottom right). Figure 2. Schematic representation of selected examples of plexcitonic colloidal nanohybrids. (a) Gold nanospheres functionalized with tetra(sulfonatophenyl)porphyrin (TPPS) (ref. 24,25) (b) gold nanourchins functionalized with tetrachloro-diethyl-di(4-sulfobutyl)benzimidazole carbocyanine (TDBC) J-aggregates [ref.26]; (c) gold nanorods functionalized with with 5,5ʹ,6,6ʹ- Tetrachloro-1,1ʹ,3,3ʹ-tetraethylbenzimidazolylcarbocyanine (JC-1) Iodide [ref.27]

Figure 3. Evidence of Rabi oscillations in plexcitonic materials. (a) Time dynamics of the transient reflectivity for a system composed of J-aggregates of cyanine dyes deposited on a gold nanoslit array. The oscillatory behavior, with periodicities corresponding to the Rabi oscillation, is shown as a function of pump-probe delay (τ). Reproduced with permission from ref.35, Copyright 2013 Springer Nature. (b) Signal decays (intensity vs time) at relevant coordinates extracted from the 2DES response of nanohybrids composed of gold nanourchins functionalized with J- aggregates. Coherent beatings are reproducibly found with a dephasing time of about 16 fs. Reproduced with permission from ref.26...

Figure 4. Fourier microscopy and spectroscopy of an organic-inorganic hybrid polariton system. (a) Schematic of Fourier microscopy setup, (b) organic-inorganic hybrid system consisting of a WS2 monolayer and thin film of TDBC, (c,e) measured dispersion in reflectance mode and (d,f) PL mode using Fourier microscopy for positive and negative exciton- cavity detuning 𝛿. Estimation of Hopfield coefficients based on dispersion measurements for (g) positive and (h) negative detuning. Adapted with permission from ref. 84 Copyright 2025 Wiley-VCH GmbH.

Figure 5. (a) ωFQFμ absorption cross sections and field-enhancement factors, (b) induced charge density calculated at the plasmon resonance frequency, and zoom maps of the region highlighted in panel b of (c) induced density and (d) electric field enhancements for nano (top) and picocavity (bottom).
